1. Introduction
1.1 Research Background and Significance
1.1.1 Development of UAV Technology and Expansion of Application Fields

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), starting from early military reconnaissance tools, have evolved over decades into intelligent aerial platforms covering both civilian and military fields. According to data from Mordor Intelligence, the global UAV market size was approximately USD 35.28 billion in 2024 and is expected to reach USD 67.64 billion by 2029, with a compound annual growth rate of 13.90%. Behind these figures lies the deep penetration of UAV application scenarios. In power line inspection, UAVs replace manual labor for fine inspection of high-voltage lines; in railway inspection, dual-UAV cooperative trajectory optimization can improve coverage in complex sections [1]; in emergency rescue, UAVs quickly enter dangerous areas after disasters, transmitting real-time scene images and life signal information [2]; in logistics delivery, SF Express's Fengyi UAVs have exceeded 20,000 daily deliveries in the Greater Bay Area, and research on urban UAV logistics route planning based on the improved A* algorithm has made progress [3]; UAV logistics is moving from pilot projects to large-scale commercial use [4]; in agricultural plant protection, as of June 2025, the global agricultural UAV fleet exceeded 500,000 units, saving approximately 330 million tons of water cumulatively, and autonomous interception planning for UAVs in low-altitude defense scenarios has also attracted attention [5]. The common requirement of these scenarios is that UAVs must autonomously, safely, and efficiently complete flight tasks in complex and variable three-dimensional spaces. Particularly, the penetration of UAVs in fields such as forest fire prevention, especially in air-ground coordination scenarios, still needs improvement. Path planning technology is the core theoretical support for achieving this goal. The above application scenarios impose rigid requirements on UAV autonomous planning capabilities. Designing scheduling for forest fire patrol and fire detection is a major issue requiring attention in the field of transportation in this information age.
1.1.2 Necessity and Challenges of 3D Path Planning in Complex Terrain Environments

The essence of path planning is to find an optimal or suboptimal collision-free path from a starting point to a target point in a given environmental model. Unlike two-dimensional planes, three-dimensional path planning needs to consider motion constraints in both horizontal and vertical directions simultaneously in continuous space, with higher-dimensional search space and greater computational complexity. In scenarios such as mountainous forest areas, urban canyons, and mining areas, UAVs face multiple challenges including terrain undulations, dense obstacles, and airflow disturbances [6]. The algorithm must achieve a balance among safety, efficiency, and path quality.

There are three difficulties. Environmental modeling is highly complex; three-dimensional terrain requires digital elevation models, and data volume increases with resolution. The search space growth problem is severe; in three-dimensional grid maps, if the map size is N×N×N, the search space grows to O(N^3), causing traditional algorithms to face significant computational overhead. UAV path planning algorithms for complex three-dimensional environments need to ensure path optimality while considering flyability and real-time computation.
1.1.3 Limitations of Existing Path Planning Algorithms in Complex 3D Scenes
Current mainstream path planning algorithms are roughly divided into three categories: graph search, sampling, and intelligent bionic methods, but each has issues in complex three-dimensional scenes.

Among graph search algorithms, the A* algorithm and its variants are widely used due to their intuitive structure and simple implementation [7]. However, when extended to three-dimensional space, both time complexity and space complexity increase sharply. In three-dimensional grid space, the number of nodes searched by traditional A* skyrockets, and it easily falls into local optima in multi-obstacle environments. Fixed step size strategies in dense obstacle environments also lead to search space explosion—experiments show that the traditional A* 26-neighbor algorithm, in a dense forest environment with 85 trees and 100 shrubs, failed to find a feasible path after exhausting 50,000 iterations, taking 417.51 seconds (see Chapter 5 for experimental details). This data reveals the limitations of fixed step size strategies in complex 3D scenes.

Sampling-based algorithms such as RRT have probabilistic completeness in high-dimensional spaces, but the random sampling mechanism leads to unstable path quality and slow convergence [8]. Existing algorithms generally suffer from low search efficiency, unsmooth paths, and difficulty in simultaneously meeting multiple objectives when handling urban three-dimensional environments.

Intelligent bionic algorithms such as genetic algorithms and ant colony algorithms show some adaptability in handling multi-objective optimization, but they have large computational loads, low convergence accuracy, and are prone to local optima [9]. These limitations restrict the flight capability of UAVs in complex terrain environments, and existing algorithms need improvement.
1.2 Domestic and International Research Status
1.2.1 Research Progress of Graph Search-based Path Planning Algorithms

Graph search algorithms are the oldest class of methods in the field of path planning with the most complete theoretical foundation. In 1959, Dijkstra proposed the classic algorithm, which diffuses outward from the starting point using a greedy strategy and guarantees to find the shortest path, but its time complexity O(|V|²) (|V| is the number of vertices in the graph) is inefficient in large-scale maps. In 1968, Hart et al. introduced a heuristic function on this basis and proposed the A* algorithm, using f(n)=g(n)+h(n) to guide the search direction. Under the condition of an admissible heuristic, A* has completeness and optimality guarantees, becoming an important cornerstone for subsequent research.

A* still has room for improvement in 3D path planning, but existing improvements mostly focus on heuristic function optimization and bidirectional search strategies, while search neighborhood design and step size adaptation mechanisms have been neglected.
1.2.2 Research Progress of Sampling-based Path Planning Algorithms

Sampling algorithms randomly sample in the configuration space to build a roadmap or tree structure, avoiding explicit modeling of obstacle areas. Such algorithms have natural advantages in high-dimensional spaces and become important tools for solving complex 3D path planning problems.

Sampling algorithms are widely used in UAV 3D path planning [10], but random sampling leads to unstable path quality, slow convergence speed, and low sampling efficiency in complex scenarios such as narrow passages. RRT* convergence speed drops sharply in high-dimensional spaces [11], which is problematic for UAV scenarios requiring high real-time performance. Compared with graph search algorithms, sampling algorithms lack strict optimality guarantees, and path quality depends on sampling density, which is a hidden danger in safety-sensitive tasks.

These defects of sampling algorithms are further amplified in complex 3D scenes. Although graph search can guarantee finding a solution, sampling algorithms struggle to converge quickly due to poor determinism.
1.2.3 Application of Intelligent Bionic Algorithms in Path Planning

Intelligent bionic algorithms simulate the collective behavior or evolutionary mechanisms of natural organisms to solve optimization problems, demonstrating unique advantages in multi-objective, non-convex, and nonlinear path planning problems.

Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) algorithm simulates the behavior mechanism of ants finding the shortest path through pheromones [12]. Dorigo et al. systematically reviewed the principles and applications of ant colony optimization algorithms, pointing out their adaptability in combinatorial optimization problems. The ant colony algorithm has strong robustness and a positive feedback mechanism, but it converges slowly, is prone to local optima, and has difficult parameter tuning [13].

Genetic Algorithm (GA) searches for optimal solutions by simulating natural selection and genetic evolution. Improved genetic algorithms have been used for multi-UAV cooperative search, improving efficiency by optimizing encoding methods and genetic operators. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm simulates the foraging behavior of bird flocks and has a relatively fast convergence speed [14]. Improved PSO algorithms have been applied to UAV 3D path planning [15], and adaptive inertial weights can balance global search and local exploitation capabilities. These intelligent bionic algorithms have achieved good results in solving specific path planning problems [16], but they generally share common problems such as high computational cost, parameter sensitivity, and limited convergence accuracy. For UAV flight missions with high real-time requirements, such algorithms are often difficult to apply directly.

However, the demonstration environments for these bionic algorithms are often overly idealized—common in papers are simple 2D scenes or small-scale 3D spaces. Once the environment becomes complex, parameter tuning becomes highly dependent on experience, lacking transferable guiding criteria.
1.2.4 Specific Challenges and Research Hotspots of UAV 3D Path Planning

UAV 3D path planning presents special technical challenges compared to general mobile robot path planning [8]. A comparative analysis of UAV path planning algorithms in urban 3D environments shows that complex 3D scenes such as urban canyons impose higher requirements on the algorithm's obstacle avoidance capability, path smoothness, and computational efficiency. UAV path planning must address several challenges: environmental uncertainty, dynamic obstacles, etc.

In recent years, multi-algorithm fusion has become popular—RRT combined with genetic algorithms, A* combined with artificial potential fields, etc., leveraging complementary advantages to improve overall performance. The application of machine learning methods is also being explored, especially the attempt of deep reinforcement learning in end-to-end path planning [17]. Deep reinforcement learning methods integrating graph neural networks and curriculum learning have demonstrated better environmental adaptability in multi-UAV path planning [18], directly learning the mapping from environmental states to control actions through neural networks. Multi-UAV cooperative path planning and real-time replanning in dynamic environments are also hot topics [19][20][21]; the latter requires algorithms to respond to environmental changes within milliseconds.

However, none of these directions solve a fundamental problem: search efficiency, path quality, and multi-objective adaptability are difficult to satisfy simultaneously. A single algorithm cannot meet multiple demands simultaneously. Making targeted improvements based on classic algorithms and then fusing other strategies is a better practice.
Table 1.1 Classification and Comparison of Major Path Planning Algorithms

Graph search algorithms have strict optimality guarantees but face search efficiency bottlenecks in 3D space—the time complexity O(b^d) of A* leads to a sharp increase in the number of nodes in 3D grids, and the 3D extension of JPS is overly complex. Sampling algorithms have good adaptability in high-dimensional spaces; RRT has a beautiful asymptotic optimality theory, but convergence speed is unstable, and sampling in narrow passages is difficult. Intelligent bionic algorithms offer flexibility in multi-objective optimization, but they have high computational overhead, lack theoretical guarantees, and parameter tuning often relies on experience. This analysis shows that a single algorithm cannot simultaneously meet the multiple demands of path planning in complex 3D environments.
1.3 Problems in Existing Research
1.3.1 Path Unsmoothness Due to Search Neighborhood Restrictions

Traditional A* algorithms in grid maps typically use a 4-neighborhood (up, down, left, right) or 8-neighborhood (including diagonals) expansion method, extending to a 6-neighborhood (face-adjacent) or 26-neighborhood (face, edge, corner-adjacent) in 3D space. However, most existing improvement methods are still limited to small neighborhood ranges, resulting in paths composed of many line segments parallel to the coordinate axes and a few diagonal segments, exhibiting obvious polyline characteristics. This unsmooth path increases flight distance and energy consumption and also causes the UAV to frequently adjust its attitude at turning points, exceeding the actual flight dynamics constraints. The polyline problem in urban low-altitude logistics scenarios affects flight safety and energy consumption. Reducing path turning points is of great significance for the actual execution of mobile robots. The 6-neighborhood in 3D space follows a Manhattan route; when moving diagonally toward the target, it can only advance in a polyline manner, and the path length deviates from the theoretical optimal value.
1.3.2 Fixed Step Size Makes It Difficult to Balance Efficiency and Safety

Most existing A* algorithm variants use a fixed step size for node expansion. This strategy has a fundamental contradiction: a smaller step size improves path accuracy but leads to a sharp increase in the number of search nodes and a significant decrease in computational efficiency; a larger step size speeds up search but may miss feasible paths in narrow passages by skipping key areas, or even collide with obstacles due to excessive step length. Hierarchical multi-granularity strategies alleviate this problem to some extent, but fixed granularity division cannot adapt to changes in terrain density. Fixed step size weighting strategies also cannot dynamically adjust based on local environmental features.

In previous debugging, I deeply experienced that with a fixed step size of 10m, the path was directly unfindable—the step was too large, and all narrow gaps were skipped. Reducing the step size to 2m allowed finding the path, but the number of iterations soared to several thousand, taking tens of seconds. A fixed step size makes it difficult to find a balance point.
1.3.3 Single Cost Function Design Unable to Meet Multi-Objective Requirements

In the evaluation function f(n)=g(n)+h(n) of the A* algorithm, g(n) is usually defined as the path length cost from the start point to the current node. In traditional design, g(n) only considers Euclidean distance or Manhattan distance, ignoring multidimensional factors such as flight safety, energy consumption, and terrain threats. In actual UAV missions, path evaluation often involves multiple or even conflicting objectives. A single distance cost cannot balance these multi-objective needs. Improved PSO algorithms attempt to introduce multi-objective weighting strategies, but the weight coefficients are fixed and cannot adapt to dynamically changing mission requirements. Genetic algorithm methods can handle multi-objective optimization, but they are computationally expensive.

The contradiction discovered in actual debugging is: when only pursuing the shortest path, the algorithm sticks close to obstacle edges; when only pursuing safety, the path becomes excessively curved. Without a cost function that can change with the environment, A* in complex 3D scenes is almost random searching.
1.4 Research Content and Innovations of This Paper
1.4.1 Research Content and Thesis Organization

This paper takes UAV path planning in complex 3D terrain environments as the research object, uses the A* algorithm as the basic framework, and addresses core problems such as restricted search neighborhoods, poor adaptability of fixed step sizes, and single cost functions. It proposes an improved A* algorithm that integrates 26-neighborhood omnidirectional expansion, precomputed threat grid lookup table adaptive step size, multi-objective dynamic weighted cost function, and path smoothing post-processing.

The thesis is divided into six chapters. Chapter 1 is an introduction, elaborating on the research background and significance, summarizing the current research status at home and abroad, analyzing existing problems, and clarifying the research content and innovations of this paper. Chapter 2 introduces related theories and technical foundations, including the mathematical description of path planning, the principles of the A* algorithm, 3D environment modeling methods, and an overview of path smoothing techniques, laying a theoretical foundation for subsequent improvements. Chapter 3 elaborates on the design scheme of the improved A* algorithm, sequentially discussing the specific implementation of the 26-neighborhood expansion strategy, the adaptive step size mechanism, the multi-objective dynamic weighted cost function, and path smoothing post-processing. Chapter 4 presents algorithm implementation and experimental analysis, including environment setup, dataset preparation, ablation experiments, and comparison experiments with classic algorithms. Chapter 5 discusses the application verification of the algorithm on an actual UAV platform and analyzes simulation flight experiment results. Chapter 6 summarizes the entire research work, outlines the main contributions, and looks forward to future directions.
1.4.2 Main Innovations
The modifications are mainly in four aspects.

Regarding search direction. Traditional A* in 3D space generally uses 6-neighborhood or 8-neighborhood expansion, limiting paths to grid axes, resulting in obvious polylines. This paper adopts 26-neighborhood omnidirectional expansion, covering all 26 adjacent nodes around a node (6 face-adjacent, 12 edge-adjacent, 8 corner-adjacent), enabling omnidirectional uniform search in 3D space. Combined with an improved heuristic function, it can explore richer path directions at the same search depth, reduce path polylines, and generate smoother paths closer to Euclidean straight lines. The 26-neighborhood makes path direction changes more continuous, reducing the frequency and magnitude of UAV attitude adjustments.

The step size strategy is another modification. A fixed step size cannot balance efficiency and safety. This paper uses a precomputed threat grid map lookup table to adaptively adjust the step size. Before searching, the threat values of the entire map are computed and built into a table. During search, a direct lookup reveals whether the area around the current node is safe, avoiding time-consuming Monte Carlo random sampling. After the lookup, the step size is dynamically adjusted: in open areas with sparse obstacles, the step size is automatically increased to accelerate search; in narrow areas with dense obstacles, the step size is automatically reduced to ensure safety. Compared with Monte Carlo estimation, the grid lookup table is deterministic, about 2.6 times faster in search speed, and without evaluation fluctuations caused by random sampling.

The cost function is also adjusted. Three objectives—path length, safety threat, and height variation—are incorporated into the actual cost g(n) of the A* algorithm, consisting of a weighted sum of distance cost, threat cost, and height penalty cost. The weights are not fixed but dynamically adjusted based on the environmental characteristics of the current node—in high-threat areas, the threat cost weight is automatically increased to prioritize safety; in low-threat open areas, the distance cost weight is increased to prioritize shorter paths. The height penalty term keeps the UAV within the low-altitude flight band of 2–6 m, avoiding unnecessary high-altitude climbs. The optimization tendency of the algorithm differs across terrain areas, which better meets the needs of actual flight missions.
Table 1.2 Comparison of Innovations in This Paper with Existing Research

Table 1.2 here shows the correspondence between the scheme in this paper and existing research. The 26-neighborhood expansion solves the polyline problem caused by traditional neighborhood restrictions; the precomputed grid lookup table replaces Monte Carlo sampling, making step size adaptation both fast and stable; the three-objective dynamic weighted cost function breaks through the limitations of a single distance cost; and the post-processing combining key point retention with B-spline curves fills the gap in path flyability. The four improvements work together: the expansion strategy provides search directions, the adaptive step size improves efficiency, the dynamic weighting balances objectives, and the smoothing post-processing ensures executability.
2. Related Theories and Technical Foundations
2.1 Overview of Path Planning Problems
2.1.1 Basic Concepts and Classification of Path Planning

Path planning is a problem in robotics, autonomous navigation, and artificial intelligence. Its essence is simple: find a feasible or optimal sequence from a start point to a goal point in a given environment. According to the degree of known environmental information and the scope of decision-making, path planning can be divided into two categories: global path planning and local path planning.

Whether obstacles in the environment change over time is another commonly used classification perspective. When obstacle positions are fixed, it is called static path planning, with difficulty in spatial search and optimization. When moving obstacles and time-varying constraints must be considered, it is called dynamic path planning, which requires higher real-time performance from the algorithm. Table 2.1 organizes path planning according to three dimensions: information completeness, environmental characteristics, and solution space.
Table 2.1 Classification System of Path Planning Problems

Global planning and local planning, static planning and dynamic planning are not mutually exclusive but can be complementary. Actual systems often adopt a layered architecture: global planning provides a rough guiding path, and local planning handles real-time dynamic obstacles. This paper focuses on global static path planning, assuming known environmental information, searching for the optimal path in a 3D grid map.
2.1.2 Mathematical Description and Constraints of 3D Path Planning

The 3D path planning problem is a constrained optimization problem. Let the 3D state space be X⊂ℝ³, where the free space is denoted as X_free and the obstacle space as X_obs, satisfying X=X_free∪X_obs and X_free∩X_obs=∅. Given a start point x_start∈X_free and a goal point x_goal∈X_free, the task of path planning is to find a continuous path σ:[0,1]→X_free in X_free such that σ(0)=x_start, σ(1)=x_goal, and the cumulative cost is minimized.
The cost function of path σ usually consists of three parts: path length, threat exposure, and smoothness.

The shorter the path, the smaller the threat, and the smoother the path, the lower the total cost. L(σ) is the path length, T(σ) is the total threat value of the path passing through threat areas, and S(σ) is the smoothness cost. w₁, w₂, w₃ are weight coefficients satisfying w₁+w₂+w₃=1. The specific values of the weights depend on mission requirements; military reconnaissance values low threat more, while logistics distribution values short distance more.
The path length L(σ) is calculated as the sum of Euclidean distances between adjacent path points:

Here p_i=(x_i,y_i,z_i) is the i-th path point, and |·|₂ denotes the Euclidean norm. Euclidean distance is chosen over Manhattan distance because the UAV can fly in any direction in 3D space; the straight-line distance between two points is the actual flight range. Manhattan distance assumes movement only along coordinate axes, which is not optimal in 3D path planning.
In actual flight, the path must also satisfy a series of kinematic and geometric constraints. Taking the UAV as an example, at least the following must be considered:
Minimum flight height constraint: The height of the path point must be higher than the local terrain height plus a safety margin:
z_terrain(x_i,y_i) is the terrain height at coordinates (x_i,y_i), and h_safe is the minimum safe flight altitude.
Maximum turning angle constraint: The angle between adjacent path segments in the horizontal plane must not exceed the maximum allowable yaw ψ_max:
a⃗_i=(x_i+1−x_i, y_i+1−y_i, z_i+1−z_i) is the direction vector of the i-th path segment.
Maximum climb angle constraint: The pitch angle change between adjacent path segments in the vertical plane must not exceed the maximum allowable climb angle θ_max:
These constraints turn path planning from a pure geometric search into a constrained optimization problem. The difficulty of solving naturally increases, but the planned paths are truly flyable.
2.1.3 Evaluation Index System for Path Planning Algorithms
The criteria for evaluating a path planning algorithm are usually assessed from four aspects: path length, flight smoothness, safety distance, and computational efficiency.
Path length is the most intuitive measure; the shorter the path, the less time and energy consumption. This paper uses a normalized index for evaluation:

L_planned is the actual length of the planned path, and L_straight is the straight-line distance from start to goal. The closer η_L is to 1, the closer the path is to theoretical optimality. This allows horizontal comparison across maps of different sizes.

A short path is only one aspect; the smoothness of the flight trajectory is equally important. Smoothness reflects the degree of curvature change along the path and affects the difficulty of UAV tracking control. If the path has all sharp turns, the flight control system will struggle to follow. Smoothness is calculated as the average turning angle at path points:
α_i is the turning angle at the i-th path point, ranging from [0,π]. The smaller the turning angle, the smoother the path.
Regarding safety. Even if the path is smooth, if it flies close to obstacles, the risk is high. Safety is measured by the minimum distance between the path and obstacles:
The larger the minimum safety distance, the safer the path. In threat environments, the cumulative threat exposure of the path passing through threat areas must also be considered.

Remaining is computational efficiency. This paper uses two indicators: running time and the number of search nodes. Running time directly reflects real-time performance, and the number of search nodes reflects the search space traversal efficiency. Under the premise of similar path quality, an algorithm that runs faster and searches fewer nodes has higher computational efficiency.
2.2 Principles of the A* Algorithm
2.2.1 Basic Idea and Evaluation Function of the A* Algorithm

Hart, Nilsson, and Raphael proposed the A* algorithm in 1968. It is a widely used heuristic search algorithm in the field of path planning. The core idea is concise: introduce heuristic information into the search process, always pushing the search toward the target direction, avoiding blind search in irrelevant areas.
The A* algorithm defines an evaluation function f(n) for each node n:

g(n) is the actual cost from the start node n_start to node n (cost-to-come), i.e., the path cost already traveled. h(n) is the heuristic estimate cost from node n to the goal node n_goal (heuristic estimate), reflecting an optimistic expectation of the remaining path.

g(n) ensures the completeness of the search process—as long as a feasible path exists, the algorithm will eventually find it. h(n) injects a sense of direction into the search, allowing the algorithm to prioritize expanding nodes that are more likely to lead to the goal. If h(n)≡0, the A* algorithm degenerates into Dijkstra's algorithm, which guarantees optimality but lacks heuristic guidance. Conversely, if h(n) exactly equals the true cost, the algorithm will go straight to the goal along the optimal path, achieving maximum efficiency.
Common heuristic functions in 3D grid maps include Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance, and Chebyshev distance. The Euclidean distance heuristic function is defined as:
It satisfies the admissibility condition, meaning its estimate will not exceed the true minimum cost from node n to the goal. This is a prerequisite for the A* algorithm to guarantee optimality.
2.2.2 Management Mechanism of the OPEN List and CLOSED Set

The execution of the A* algorithm relies on two data structures working together. The OPEN list stores candidate nodes that have been discovered but not yet expanded, sorted by f value from smallest to largest. This ensures that the node taken from the head of the list each time is the one most promising to lead to the goal. The CLOSED set records nodes that have been expanded and processed, preventing duplicate expansion and avoiding the search falling into infinite loops.

The basic flow of the A* algorithm consists of several steps. Put the start node into the OPEN list with g value initialized to 0. Then enter a loop: each time take the node n with the smallest f value from the OPEN list. If n is the goal node, the algorithm ends and backtracks the path. If not, move n into the CLOSED set, then traverse all its neighbor nodes m. For each neighbor, calculate the tentative g value as g(n)+c(n,m), where c(n,m) is the movement cost from n to m. If m has not been visited, or the newly computed g value is smaller, update m's parent pointer and g value, and put it into the OPEN list (or reorder).

The implementation efficiency of the OPEN list affects the performance of the A* algorithm. In engineering, a min-heap is commonly used as the underlying data structure, and the time complexity of taking the minimum value is O(log|V|), where |V| is the total number of nodes in the graph.
2.2.3 Time Complexity and Space Complexity Analysis of the A* Algorithm

The time complexity of the A* algorithm is closely related to the quality of the heuristic function. In the worst case, when the heuristic function fails, A* may need to expand all nodes in the graph, with a time complexity of O(|E|), where |E| is the number of edges in the graph. The worst-case time complexity in a three-dimensional grid map is O(N³), where N is the grid dimension.

In practical applications, the time complexity of the A* algorithm is often written as O(b^ε · d). b is the branching factor, d is the depth of the optimal solution, and ε is the effectiveness coefficient of the heuristic function. When h(n)≡0, ε=1, corresponding to the worst case of Dijkstra's algorithm; when h(n)=h*(n), ε=0, and the algorithm only expands nodes along the optimal path.

The space complexity of the A* algorithm mainly arises from the storage of the OPEN list and the CLOSED set. In the worst case, all nodes must be stored simultaneously, resulting in a space complexity of O(|V|). In large-scale 3D environments, space complexity is the main bottleneck of A*, which has also led to low-memory variants such as IDA*.
Table 2.2 Comparative Analysis of the A* Algorithm and Dijkstra's Algorithm

The core difference between the two lies in the search strategy. A*, through an appropriate heuristic function, compresses the search space from the entire graph to the funnel area between the start point and the goal, reducing the time complexity from O(b^d) to O(b^ε d) (ε<1). Dijkstra's algorithm, lacking heuristic guidance, can only expand isotropically like ripples, which becomes impractical when the number of nodes is large. This is also the reason why this paper selects the A* algorithm as the basis for improvement.
2.3 Introduction of Commonly Used Comparison Algorithms
2.3.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm and Greedy Best-First Search

Dijkstra's algorithm can be regarded as an A* algorithm with heuristic function h(n)≡0, guaranteeing optimality but lacking search direction guidance, resulting in low efficiency in 3D environments. Greedy best-first search goes to the other extreme, retaining only the heuristic term f(n)=h(n). The search is greedy but the path quality is not guaranteed. In this paper's experiments, GBFS is introduced as a baseline for comparison to verify the balanced design between g(n) and h(n) in the improved A*.
2.3.2 Principle of the RRT* Algorithm

The RRT* algorithm was proposed by Karaman and Frazzoli in 2011. It introduces a rewiring optimization mechanism on the basis of the RRT algorithm, achieving asymptotic optimality. Unlike deterministic graph-search-based algorithms such as A*, RRT* belongs to sampling-based probabilistic algorithms, which gradually build a search tree through random sampling to approximate the optimal path.
The workflow of the RRT* algorithm can be divided into six steps:
• Random sampling: Uniformly and randomly sample a state point x_rand in the free space X_free.
• Nearest neighbor search: Find the node x_near in the constructed search tree that is closest to x_rand.
• Tree extension: Extend from x_near toward x_rand by a fixed step size η to obtain a new node x_new.
• Collision detection: Check whether the path segment from x_near to x_new collides with obstacles; if so, discard this node.
• Optimal parent node selection: Search all nodes in the neighborhood centered at x_new with radius r_n, and select the node that minimizes the cumulative cost to x_new as the parent node.
• Rewiring optimization: Check whether each node in the neighborhood can obtain a smaller cumulative cost through x_new; if so, update the parent pointer.

The RRT* algorithm has two key theoretical properties. First, probabilistic completeness: if a solution exists, the probability of finding the solution tends to 1 as the number of iterations approaches infinity. Second, asymptotic optimality: as the number of iterations increases, the path cost found by the algorithm almost surely converges to the optimal value. The neighborhood radius r_n is taken as n is the current number of iterations, d is the dimension of the state space, and γ is a constant related to the geometric characteristics of the space.

The RRT* algorithm has stronger scalability in high-dimensional continuous spaces than grid search algorithms, but its convergence speed is slow. The nature of random sampling means that the path obtained in each run may be different, and the path quality is not stable.
2.3.3 Artificial Potential Field Method and Other Methods

The artificial potential field method guides a moving body toward a target by constructing virtual gravitational and repulsive fields. It is simple to compute and has good real-time performance, but suffers from local optimal traps and target unreachability, making it difficult to use independently in complex 3D environments. Intelligent bio-inspired algorithms simulate collective behaviors in nature to solve optimization problems [27], offering some flexibility in multi-objective path planning [28], but they have large computational requirements, sensitivity to parameters, and limited convergence accuracy [29], making them unsuitable for UAV flight tasks with high real-time demands.
2.3.4 Overview of Directions for Improving the A* Algorithm
Focusing on the limitations of the A* algorithm in complex 3D environments, existing research primarily explores improvements from several directions.

Traditional 6-neighborhood or 8-neighborhood searches generate many polyline paths in 3D space. Liu Wenjian et al., addressing the directional constraints of the JPS algorithm in 3D extension, proposed the TDC-JPS improvement strategy [22], which reduces unnecessary node expansions through 3D pruning rules. Xi Lele et al., in the AHA algorithm, introduced a 26-neighborhood omnidirectional expansion, improving the continuity of path directions [30]. These works indicate that expanding the neighborhood search range helps reduce path polylines.

A fixed step size strategy faces a dilemma between efficiency and safety in complex terrain. Cheng Qing et al., for 3D mountainous terrain scenes, proposed an improved LO algorithm by integrating crayfish optimization and Lévy flight strategies to achieve dynamic adjustment of the search step size [24], achieving good path planning results on Gaussian terrain. Han Tao et al., in an improved sand cat swarm optimization algorithm, designed a four-objective cost function combined with an adaptive step size strategy to enhance the stability of path planning for plant protection UAVs in complex farmland environments [25]. These works demonstrate that adaptive step size is effective in different scenarios.

Traditional A* considers only distance cost and cannot balance multiple requirements such as safety and energy consumption. Fan Longge et al., addressing multi-objective UAV path planning, designed a comprehensive cost function including path length, threat avoidance, and smoothness [31], solving the Pareto front using an improved salp swarm algorithm. Zhang Yuwei et al., in an improved goose optimization algorithm, introduced a hybrid framework incorporating path smoothness, curvature change, and acceleration continuity into the evaluation metrics [26]. These works provide reference for the design of multi-objective cost functions.

The above improvement directions each have their focus, but the synergistic optimization of neighborhood expansion, step size adaptation, and multi-objective weighting has not been systematically studied. Existing improvements mostly focus on a single aspect. This paper combines four improvements—26-neighborhood expansion, grid table lookup adaptive step size, dynamic weighted cost function, and path smoothing post-processing—to form the EA* algorithm, with the detailed design presented in Chapter 4. Chapter 3 first constructs the simulation environment.
2.4 Environmental Modeling Methods in Path Planning
2.4.1 Grid Map Modeling Method

The grid map is a commonly used environmental representation method in path planning. The idea is to discretize continuous space into regular grid cells, each assigned a state attribute. In 2D path planning, the environment is divided into an M×N square grid, where each grid's state is typically taken as free (0) or obstacle (1).

3D grid maps extend this concept to three-dimensional space, dividing the environment into an M×N×P set of cubic voxels. The voxel coordinates (i, j, k) corresponding to any spatial point (x, y, z) are calculated as follows: where r is the voxel resolution, ⌊·⌋ is the floor operation, and (x_min, y_min, z_min) are the coordinates of the spatial origin.

The selection of grid resolution r requires trade-offs. The higher the resolution (smaller r), the more accurately the geometric details of the environment are represented, but the number of grids increases cubically, increasing the computational burden of path planning. If the resolution is too low, narrow passages may be misjudged, losing feasible paths. The actual choice depends on the complexity of the environment and the available computational resources.
2.4.2 Threat Field Modeling Method

Threat sources in path planning (radar detection zones, air defense fire coverage areas, fire spreading zones, etc.) can be abstracted as scalar fields to represent the degree of danger. A commonly used model is the distance-based threat field, where the farther from the threat source, the lower the threat intensity. When multiple threat sources coexist, the total threat field can be combined by superposition or by taking the maximum value. In path planning, the threat cost is added to the g(n) of the A* algorithm, so the search automatically favors low-threat areas.
2.4.3 Terrain Modeling Method

3D path planning requires terrain information to determine flight altitude and obstacle avoidance strategies. Common methods include Gaussian function modeling, digital elevation models (DEM), and Perlin noise synthesis. The Gaussian function constructs mountainous terrain by superimposing multiple bell-shaped surfaces, with controllable parameters, suitable for simulation testing. DEM uses actual measured elevation data, offering high accuracy and is more commonly used in actual flight mission planning. Perlin noise generates terrain details through gradient noise, suitable for superimposing micro-undulations. Zhu Pei et al., in a study on multi-UAV path planning for plateau forest firefighting, used digital elevation models to construct 3D terrain [32], indicating that combining Gaussian functions with real terrain data is feasible. This paper synthesizes simulation terrain using Gaussian peaks and Perlin noise, providing macro-geomorphic features and micro-surface details to serve as test scenarios for algorithm validation.
2.5 Chapter Summary

This chapter focuses on the theoretical foundations of 3D path planning for UAVs. It introduces the basic concepts, classifications, and mathematical descriptions of path planning problems, clarifying the scope of global static path planning research. The key content includes the principle of the A* algorithm, cost function design, OPEN/CLOSED management mechanism, admissibility and optimality proofs, and complexity analysis. The principles and characteristics of GBFS, RRT*, and the artificial potential field method are also introduced. An overview of improvements to the A* algorithm in directions such as neighborhood expansion, step size adaptation, and multi-objective cost functions is provided. Environmental representation methods such as grid maps, threat fields, and terrain modeling are also covered. The A* algorithm faces problems such as limited neighborhoods, fixed step sizes, and single cost functions in complex 3D environments. Chapter 3 constructs a 3D environment model for the mountain forest firefighting scenario.
3. Environmental Modeling and Threat Assessment

The performance of a path planning algorithm is related to the test environment. This chapter, targeting the mountain forest firefighting scenario, constructs a simulation environment using 3D terrain with multiple threat sources for subsequent verification of the improved A* algorithm.
3.1 Construction of 3D Terrain Model
3.1.1 Mountain Terrain Modeling Method Based on Gaussian Function Superposition

The Gaussian function has a characteristic: its bell-shaped curve closely resembles the uplift shape of natural mountain peaks. This paper selects it for terrain modeling because the surface decays smoothly outward from a central point, which matches the trend of real mountains. In the field of terrain simulation, this method is widely used. By combining multiple Gaussian functions with different heights, central positions, and spreads, a continuous terrain surface with multi-peak structures and natural transitions can be generated. Moreover, during literature review, this function was frequently encountered, so it was naturally chosen; consequently, noise mountains are adopted in subsequent optimization. Otherwise, the Perlin noise mountain might have been preferred, but currently the two can coexist entirely.

Let the two-dimensional horizontal coordinate system be (x, y) ∈ [0, Lx] × [0, Ly]. The Gaussian terrain function for the i-th mountain peak is defined as: where h_i is the peak height of the i-th peak in meters; (a_i, b_i) are the projection coordinates of the peak center on the horizontal plane; and k_i controls the shape of the peak, affecting the slope and the width of the base. The smaller k_i is, the steeper and more concentrated the peak; the larger k_i is, the flatter and more extended the peak.

In a multi-peak scenario, the terrain height is taken as the pointwise maximum of all peak functions, rather than the algebraic sum. Initially, additive superposition was used in the experiment, but when two peaks were close together, the height in the middle area was abnormally elevated, creating a raised area that does not exist in reality. Moreover, this approach overlaps conceptually with Perlin noise and performed poorly, so the maximum value was chosen instead, making the terrain appear richer.

Assuming there are N_m peaks in the scene, the composite terrain function is: Each peak represents an independent terrain uplift, separated by valleys or ridges. The height is dominated by the nearest uplift, which conforms to the physical logic of real terrain.
3.1.2 Multi-peak Terrain Parameter Design and Scene Configuration

The experimental scene in this chapter is a square area of 200m × 200m, with a vertical range of 0–40m, simulating eight peaks. In fact, the algorithm iteration initially chose 100m × 100m, but was eventually expanded to 200m. The reason for this choice is that many conventional papers use a 100-meter perimeter as a standard. Considering the lack of confidence in computer performance, this choice was made initially; however, as the experiment progressed, especially after comparing with other algorithms, good quantitative results were obtained, so the map size was ultimately enlarged. In addition, obstacles were upgraded qualitatively: bushes, trees, mountains, and fires were included to simulate real-world scenes as closely as possible, ensuring a degree of algorithm reliability. Moreover, the approach of the paper is more abstract: for example, a bush is not merely defined as a bush but represents a complex forest floor scenario. In such scenarios, the forest floor is certainly not smooth but rough (i.e., the noise mountain used in the experiment). In reality, forests also do not have large expanses of bare ground; they are mostly overgrown with weeds, which represents the lower bound.

The heights of the eight peaks are set between 15–25m, corresponding to the level of low mountains and hills. This range is not based on reliable data but on visual data. The common Gaussian mountains tend to be steeper overall, but in terms of visualization, especially on this relatively small map, they appear quite strange—perhaps due to too many peaks. However, if there were only one or two peaks, that would also seem inappropriate. Therefore, to demonstrate the algorithm's performance, and for a more important reason—the flight altitude of a UAV has a theoretical upper limit; in our scenario, under normal algorithm conditions, a UAV would not choose to cross over a peak, especially such a high one, so lower altitudes may become the choice. In other words, high mountains are more like inaccessible forbidden zones where the algorithm itself would not work. Hence, this is a reasonable idealization correction.

The eight peaks cover most of the scene. Peak 3 is the highest at 25.0m, located at (90,110), serving as the main peak. In the northwest (50,50), Peak 1 is 22.0m, blocking the entrance direction. In the northeast (130,70), Peak 2 is 18.0m, connecting with the main peak to form a northeast ridge. In the southwest (40,150), Peak 5 is 20.0m. The remaining four: Peak 4 in the southeast (160,140) at 15.0m; Peak 6 in the north (140,30) at 16.0m; Peak 7 in the east (100,40) at 19.0m; Peak 8 in the west (60,130) at 17.0m. They block the flight path from different directions.

k_i takes values from 0.006 to 0.009, corresponding to different terrain types from steep peaks to gentle hills. The terrain has sufficient height variation to test the algorithm's obstacle-crossing capability while leaving valley passages that can be traversed. If the scene is too enclosed, the planning problem may have no solution.

The UAV start point is set at (10,10,5), located in the low-altitude area in the southwest corner; the goal point is set at (170,170,8), near the edge of the fire zone in the northeast. The start altitude was initially set to 8m. The problem encountered was that, whether due to penalty failure or other reasons, the algorithm did not navigate between obstacles and trees as intended. Therefore, the altitude was reduced to 5m, forcing the algorithm to find the initial heading at a lower altitude level, thereby reflecting the realism of low-altitude planning. Moreover, after changing the start and goal altitudes, the algorithm's performance showed good results. Another point considered is that the sensor data transmitted—specifically, the goal data—should not be at a location in the sky, which is unreasonable. So, keeping the goal close to the ground is also more practical.

In addition to Gaussian peaks, this chapter also introduces Perlin noise to superimpose micro-undulations. The noise parameters are as follows: spatial scaling factor scale=0.03, number of octaves octaves=4, persistence persistence=0.5, global amplitude amplitude=5.0m. These numbers were not determined arbitrarily. scale=0.03 corresponds to a noise period of approximately 33m, resulting in about 5–6 undulation cycles on a 200m map, which looks natural visually. octaves=4 superimposes a base layer plus three detail layers, and persistence=0.5 causes the amplitude of high-frequency components to halve layer by layer.
3.1.3 Terrain Boundary Handling and Collision Detection Mechanism

Terrain computation is restricted to the rectangular area x∈[0,200], y∈[0,200]. For coordinate points beyond the boundary, the terrain function returns negative infinity, thus preventing the UAV from data overflow or, in other words, code escape. This point, including the subsequent issue of negative ground values causing the UAV to burrow into the ground, was difficult for me to explain, but after multiple rounds of thought, a modification direction was found.

Collision detection is divided into two levels. Point collision detection determines whether a single point P=(p_x, p_y, p_z) is within the valid flight space: The first two conditions handle boundaries, and the third prevents ground collision. D_safe is the safe distance threshold, whose physical meaning and value basis will be discussed in Section 3.3. Only when all three conditions are satisfied is the point considered feasible.

Segment collision detection checks whether the line segment between two path nodes penetrates the terrain. There was a pitfall here: initially, only the endpoints were checked in this experiment, and paths were found to go directly through mountain interiors—paths passing straight through mountains. The reason is that intermediate points along the segment may pass through obstacles; safe endpoints do not guarantee a safe entire segment. The solution is to uniformly sample N_sample intermediate points along the segment and check each one. Let the segment start point be P_s and the end point be P_e; the j-th sampling point is: This also illustrates that logical rigor often still has loopholes in experiments; therefore, this experiment also exposed a point: the algorithm itself cannot guarantee that path segments do not penetrate obstacles; it relies on the newly added patch to ensure non-penetration.
When N_sample ≥ 10, the sampling accuracy is basically sufficient to cover the main undulations of the terrain surface, and the detection reliability is acceptable in actual verification.
3.1.4 Perlin Noise Terrain Detail Layer

Purely Gaussian peak-generated terrain has a problem: apart from the mountains, the rest is flat ground with a minimum z-coordinate of zero, which is not realistic. Therefore, Perlin noise was added to impose micro-undulations on the macroscopic terrain.

Perlin noise generates smooth random textures based on gradient interpolation and is widely used in computer graphics. A MOPSO path planning study published by MDPI in 2025 also adopted a similar approach: superimposing controllable-amplitude noise components on a base terrain to simulate the impact of micro-unevenness on aircraft trajectories. The general form of the synthetic terrain formula given in that study is: This chapter borrows this idea, combining macroscopic Gaussian peaks and Perlin noise: where max{z_i(x,y)} is the pointwise maximum terrain of Gaussian peaks defined in Section 3.1.1, Perlin(x,y) is the 2D Perlin noise function, and α is the noise amplitude coefficient. Perlin noise is generated by superposition of multiple octaves, each with different spatial frequency and amplitude, with the amplitude of high-frequency components decaying according to the persistence coefficient.

A debugging detail is added here: α was initially set to only 3.0m, resulting in a 1.5% undulation ratio on a 200m-scale map, making the ground appear too flat in 3D plots. After increasing it to 5.0m, the micro-undulations became somewhat clearer in 3D visualization.

The return value of Perlin noise can be positive or negative. After superposition, it may cause the local ground height to become negative, and the ground should not be below sea level. Therefore, a non-negative processing step is added to the terrain calculation: Z(x,y)=max(Z(x,y),0). With this processing, α=5.0m will not produce unreasonable depressions, and to some extent, solves the UAV ground-penetration issue.
3.2 Threat Source Modeling
3.2.1 Continuous Fire Threat Model: Elliptical Spread Pattern

The fire zone is an intuitive external threat in mountain forest firefighting scenarios. Early studies often simplified fire to a few discrete point heat sources, but the spread pattern of a real wildfire is usually elliptical or irregular continuous distribution. Under the influence of wind, slope, and fuel distribution, the fire front extends faster along the long axis than the short axis. The continuous ellipse model is closer to real wildfire spread than discrete point heat sources.

This chapter simplifies the fire zone as an elliptical area on the horizontal plane, with the ellipse center C_fire=(c_x, c_y), semi-major axis r_x, semi-minor axis r_y, and the angle between the major axis and the positive x-axis is θ. When calculating the normalized distance from any horizontal position (p_x, p_y) to the fire zone boundary, first translate the coordinates to the ellipse center, then rotate to the principal axis coordinate system: The ellipse normalized distance is defined as: d_e < 1 indicates a point inside the ellipse, d_e = 1 on the boundary, and d_e > 1 outside. Based on this distance, the fire threat value T_fire(P) is defined as a piecewise function: Inside the ellipse, the threat is infinite and cannot be crossed; within the R_decay range outside the boundary, the threat linearly decays from 1 to 0, forming a danger transition zone; beyond that, the threat is zero. The fire parameters in this chapter: center (165,165), semi-major axis r_x=25m, semi-minor axis r_y=18m, rotation angle θ=30°, decay distance R_decay=15m. This configuration simulates a wildfire in the southeast area spreading in the northeast–southwest direction, covering an area of approximately 1413m², which is roughly comparable to the initial spread scale of a real forest fire.

The position of the goal point (170,170,8) was also adjusted. Initially, the goal coordinates were not coordinated with the fire zone center and nearly fell inside the ellipse. Later, the goal was kept near the fire zone but not inside it, so the path can approach the fire zone edge for reconnaissance but cannot start or end inside the fire zone, which is more reasonable.
3.2.2 Tree Obstacle Model: Cylindrical Trunk + Spherical Crown

Trees in the forest are obstacles that low-altitude drones must avoid. Traditional methods simplify trees as cylinders or cones, which lack precision. This chapter adopts a combined model of a cylindrical trunk and a spherical crown, which better approximates the three-dimensional occupancy of real trees and is also applicable to densely forested areas with thick canopies.

Upon inquiry, real forestry survey data from China supports this simplification: typical deciduous and coniferous trees in the Funiu Mountains of Henan, the Shennongjia area of Hubei, and the Changbai Mountain region of Northeast China have heights of 8–25 m. According to forestry survey data, crown parameters vary within a certain range.

The modeling parameters for each tree include: horizontal position (xt, yt), tree height h, trunk radius rt, crown radius rc, and crown base height hb. The tree height follows a truncated normal distribution h ∼ N(10.0, 2.5²), truncated to the interval [5.0, 18.0] m; the crown radius and crown base height are randomly generated proportionally to the tree height:
The trunk radius rt is taken as 0.2–0.5 m, consistent with the range of common tree diameter at breast height.

The crown base height ratio of 0.25–0.55 was not fixed initially. It was first set to 0.45–0.70, resulting in excessive clearance: for a 10 m tree, the crown base was at 4.5–7 m, leaving the area below 4.5 m completely empty. The path could fly freely in this clearance without any effect of weaving through crown gaps. After adjusting to 0.25–0.55, the crown base was pressed down to 2.5–5.5 m, compressing the low-altitude space, forcing the algorithm to detour around crowns. However, even this did not achieve the desired effect well. After considering the possible real flight of drones and consulting materials, it was found that detouring in non-dead-end environments is correct, but ground-level elements also need consideration. This led to the addition of shrubs for more detail. Compared to the fire-and-mountain model constructed in earlier experiments, this environment modeling is more consistent with real data. Real data was also considered in the experiments, but real geographic data at this scale is unnecessary for simulation, because geographic information data essentially records ground positions. Based on research and inference, choosing real data would only result in a model recalculated after removing sea level. Rather than obsessing over pixel-level model realism, I chose to add Perlin mountain noise.

A total of 85 trees are arranged in a 200 m × 200 m scene. The distribution is not simple random but a strategic layout with dense diagonal channels and sparse background. The reason is: the drone flies from (10,10) to (170,170), and the shortest path roughly follows the northeast-southwest diagonal. If the trees are uniformly sparse, this is deliberately designed—in computer science terms, it might be called stress testing, which is what I expected, because there is a critical point between sparse and dense. In random terrain, it is difficult to find a map that is both sufficiently representative and that reflects algorithm characteristics. Therefore, the experiment made the central shrubs and trees denser, expecting to see if the flight trajectory detours. However, even increasing density did not ensure good detours, because detouring requires the central area to be impassable. Such an experiment seemed somewhat unrealistic, so I chose to reduce density but still ensure that obstacles along the central diagonal are denser. Thus, about 60% (51 trees) of the 85 trees are densely distributed along the diagonal channel, and the remaining 40% (34 trees) are sparsely scattered as background.

Specific generation method: the diagonal starts at S = (10,10) and ends at G = (170,170), with direction vector d⃗ = G - S. Dense trees are randomly and uniformly sampled along the diagonal parameter t ∈ [0.2, 0.85] to obtain points Pdiag = S + t·d⃗, then offset perpendicularly with a normal distribution N(0, 12²). The t range [0.2, 0.85] avoids accumulating too many trees near the start and end points, ensuring the takeoff and landing segments can be flown through; σ = 12 m creates a dense obstacle band about 24 m (2σ) wide along the diagonal. Sparse background trees are randomly generated uniformly across the map, but subject to two constraints: distance from the start or end point is not less than 20 m; distance from the diagonal centerline is not less than 30 m, preventing sparse trees from falling into the dense area and disrupting the corridor structure. This also avoids physical walls in sparse environments. Since we chose not to add obstacles, if the algorithm can find the correct path under pressure, the experiment is successful.

The tree threat model also underwent another adjustment. An early version used global uniform parameters Dsafe = 2.0 m and Dwarn = 8.0 m. The total threat radius for a single tree was about 12 m, which was too sparse on a 200 m map, causing the threat network to fail to link. Additionally, the maximum flight height was initially set to 50 m (later reduced to 20 m to strengthen height penalties), and the path flew straight over all obstacles at 10–15 m altitude, with no low-altitude traversal at all. Therefore, the tree threat model was changed to independent gradual attenuation parameters: 2–5 m outside the trunk surface, 1.5–4 m outside the crown surface, making the threat gradient steeper. Meanwhile, the threat weight WT was increased from 0.15 to 0.25, the maximum flight height was compressed to 15 m, and a threat extension zone of 3 m above the crown top was added. After the modification, the path height was suppressed to 5–8 m, and behaviors of detouring around crown gaps were clearly visible. It is unclear whether this is somewhat similar to 2D path planning, but in reality, this is to make the algorithm demonstrate performance. Thus, at steep areas, the drone clearly maintains a safe height from the ground.
3.2.3 Shrub Threat Model: Low-Altitude Layered Cylindrical Obstacles

In a real forest, there are not only tall trees but also a shrub layer (0.5–3 m) under the forest, which is also an obstacle for low-altitude drone flight. Initially, this chapter only had 85 tall trees and 8 hills, leaving a large empty space from the crown base to the ground. When the drone flew at 2–6 m altitude, there was little threat pressure below, allowing the algorithm to arbitrarily choose altitude, lacking the tension of forced precise traversal.

This is the problem of excessive emptiness in low-altitude space due to the absence of a shrub layer. After adding this layer, 100 shrub clumps were introduced into the scene. Shrubs are simplified as cylinders: horizontal position (xs, ys), height hs, base radius rs, occupying an upright cylinder from the ground to height hs. Compared to the complex combined model for trees, the shrub model is much simpler, with low height and no need to consider spherical crown expansion. Parameters: hs ∼ Uniform(0.8, 3.0) m, rs ∼ Uniform(1.2, 3.5) m. The upper limit of 3.0 m prevents shrubs from extending into the crown area, maintaining vertical layering.

The distribution strategy for 100 shrub clumps is similar to that for trees, but more concentrated: about 65% (65 clumps) are densely distributed along the diagonal channel, and 35% (35 clumps) are sparsely scattered. The diagonal parameter range for dense shrubs is t ∈ [0.15, 0.90], with a perpendicular offset standard deviation σ = 8 m (more concentrated than the 12 m for trees), making shrubs closer to the main flight corridor, making low-altitude traversal more difficult. Sparse shrubs must avoid a buffer zone of 12 m around the start and end points, and must be at least 25 m from the diagonal centerline. The random seed for shrubs is RANDOMSEED + 1000 to avoid overlap with tree positions.

The threat degree Tshrub(P) for a shrub is determined by horizontal distance and vertical height. Let the horizontal distance between the drone and the shrub centerline be d_horizontal, and the relative ground height be pzrel = pz - Zterrain(xs, ys). Then:

Within 3 m above the shrub top, and within 1 m horizontally from the cylinder surface, it is a collision exclusion zone; within the range of 1–3 m, the threat linearly decays to 0; beyond a horizontal distance of rs + 3.0 m or a height above hs + 3.0 m, the threat disappears. This reflects the real scenario: low shrubs only threaten ground-hugging or low-altitude flight; the drone only needs to fly above the shrub by more than 3 m, without large horizontal avoidance like for trees.
3.2.4 Comprehensive Threat Assessment Model: Physical Meaning and Rationality Analysis of the Maximum Value Strategy

When multiple threat sources exist simultaneously, the comprehensive threat at a point takes the maximum value among all types of threats. Suppose the scene has Nt trees and Ns shrub clumps. The comprehensive threat at position P is defined as:

Tmountain(P) is terrain danger (defined in Section 3.3), Tfire(P) is fire threat, Tshrub(P) is the maximum threat among all shrubs, and Ttree,k(P) is the threat of the k-th tree. The comprehensive threat is finally clipped to [0, 1]: Tfinal(P) = min(T(P), 1.0).
Tree threat is calculated piecewise based on the spatial relationship between the drone and the trunk or crown:

Trunk part: dtree is the shortest distance to the cylinder surface. Safety distance Dsafetrunk = 5.0 m (threat zero 5 m outside the trunk surface), near distance Dneartrunk = 2.0 m (maximum threat within 2 m outside the trunk surface). Crown part: dtree is the shortest distance to the sphere surface. Dsafecrown = 4.0 m, Dnearcrown = 1.5 m. The trunk safety zone is larger than the crown's because there may be branches and windthrow risks near the trunk, requiring sufficient horizontal avoidance distance.

There is also a threat extension zone above the crown. The top branches of the crown extend outward, and the rotor airflow also disturbs the surroundings. Therefore, the area within 3 m directly above the top of the crown sphere is defined as a threat attenuation zone. Let the drone be directly above the crown, with vertical distance dabove from the crown top. Then:

When dabove = 0, threat = 1, connecting with the sphere surface; linearly decays upward, and becomes zero above 3 m. This mechanism comes from a problem discovered during actual debugging: in early versions, anything above 4 m above the crown top was considered safe, so the algorithm preferred to raise the path near the crown top, skimming over it. On a 2D threat heatmap, it appeared as crossing high-threat areas. After adding the 3 m threat extension above, the path either rises further or detours laterally, ensuring 3D safety.

The maximum value is chosen here because if a point has a very high danger, the drone will naturally not pass through it. However, if a seemingly less dangerous point, due to superposition, causes the path to detour, this actual performance is observed from the heatmap. I was puzzled to see a simulation environment with nothing at a certain location but the path circling around it. Upon in-depth inquiry, I learned that summation should not be used here. However, before consulting data and experiments, most would choose summation, which seems reasonable, but actually is not.

Additionally, there was a pitfall in the path smoothing stage. After B-spline curve smoothing, the minimum path height became -3.17 m, meaning the drone flew below ground. After much investigation, it was found that scipy's splprep/splev produced undershoot near the endpoints, with interpolation points below the minimum of all control points. The original path (after jump-point greedy simplification) had a height range of [5.00, 10.66] m, which was fine; the problem only occurred after B-spline smoothing. Before returning from the smooth_path function, I added height clipping: if below ground height + Dsafe, raise to safe height; if above Hmax, lower to maximum allowed height. After fixing, the smooth path height range became [2.00, 11.66] m, solving the negative height problem.
3.3 Terrain Danger Assessment Model
3.3.1 Segmented Terrain Danger Model Based on Vertical Distance

Terrain danger differs from fixed obstacles. Mountains provide continuous height constraints, requiring the drone to always maintain a safe vertical distance from the ground. This chapter divides the vertical clearance between the drone's current position and the ground directly below into several intervals to assess terrain danger.
For any three-dimensional point P = (px, py, pz), the ground height directly below is given by the terrain function Zterrain(px, py). The vertical clearance distance is:

Terrain danger Tmountain(P) is divided into three intervals based on dsurface. When clearance is less than Dsafe, the drone is in a danger zone, threat infinite; when clearance is between Dsafe and Dwarn, threat linearly decreases from 1 to 0; when clearance is greater than Dwarn, terrain threat is zero.
3.3.2 Physical Meaning and Setting of Safety Distance and Warning Distance Parameters

Safety distance Dsafe is the closest distance the drone is allowed to approach the ground. This parameter must consider the drone's size, flight control accuracy, attitude shifts due to gusts, sensor errors, etc. Small multi-rotor UAVs in mountainous environments are affected by gusts, with significant vertical position fluctuations, requiring sufficient safety margin. Additionally, modeling errors in the terrain function and increased effective height due to ground vegetation cover lead to taking Dsafe = 2.0 m. Although the initial experiment aimed to explore optimal data, in reality, such unnecessary operations may be unnecessary in this paper. Based on results from multiple papers, settings are either engineering or reasonable data. The paper does not explain why, for example, the fire hazard coefficient for a sphere is inversely proportional to the fourth power of distance; it can only be understood that this exponent emphasizes that closer means more dangerous. Therefore, many data are not necessarily derived from various papers, but non-critical data are designed from an engineering reasonable angle and experimental purpose (e.g., the height setting of Gaussian hills discussed earlier).

Warning distance Dwarn determines where terrain threat starts to take effect. If set too small, terrain cannot constrain the path, and planning results may frequently skim the ground; if set too large, the flyable area is too small, and the planning problem may become unsolvable. This chapter takes Dwarn = 8.0 m. In the transition zone of 2–8 m from the ground, the path is repelled by terrain; above 8 m, it is not constrained by terrain. The 6 m transition band between Dsafe and Dwarn provides operational room for the cruising altitude of multi-rotor UAVs. This is a physical consideration and a preference for integers. In reality, setting values like 1.99 would not affect the final experimental results.

This chapter also adds absolute height constraints. Maximum flight height Hmax = 15.0 m forces the drone to stay low, preventing it from flying high over all obstacles. Minimum flight height Hmin = 2.0 m, consistent with Dsafe, ensures a safe ground clearance. Overall, the drone is suitable for flying at 2–6 m. This range lies within the terrain threat transition zone and is far from the maximum height limit, leaving some margin for emergency climbs.

The literature also mentions similar heights. Agricultural plant protection UAVs typically operate at 2–3.5 m above the crop canopy. In this chapter's scene, mountain peaks reach 25 m, and with a 15 m maximum height limit, the flight altitude band is set at 2–6 m, balancing low-altitude reconnaissance and obstacle maneuvering.
3.3.3 Collision Detection Algorithm: Implementation of Point Detection and Line Segment Detection
Collision detection determines whether a candidate path is feasible. This chapter implements two detection modes.

Point collision detection determines whether a single position P = (px, py, pz) is feasible. The horizontal coordinates must be within map boundaries, i.e., px ∈ [0, Lx], py ∈ [0, Ly], with Lx = Ly = 200 m; exceeding boundaries counts as collision. Height must satisfy Hmin ≤ pz ≤ Hmax; violation also counts as collision. Then calculate the ground height Zterrain(px, py) at that horizontal position. If pz < Zterrain(px, py) + Dsafe, it means the drone has penetrated the ground safety zone, counts as collision. Then calculate the normalized distance de to the elliptical fire field; if de < 1 (inside the fire field), it also counts as collision. Then iterate through all trees, checking whether the point is inside a trunk cylinder or crown sphere; if so, collision. Then iterate through all shrub clumps, checking whether the point is inside a shrub cylinder (within shrub height range and horizontal distance less than shrub radius); if so, collision. Only if all checks pass is the point safe.

Line segment collision detection extends point detection, checking whether the line segment between two path nodes is safe. Implementation is consistent with Section 3.1.3: uniformly sample the segment, with Nsample increasing with segment length; long segments have more sample points. Each sample point sequentially undergoes point collision detection. If all sample points pass, the entire segment is traversable; if any sample point collides, the segment is considered collided. To save time, an AABB bounding box pre-filter can be performed: check whether the axis-aligned bounding box of the segment endpoints intersects with various threat regions; if not, skip detailed sampling.

Point detection and line segment detection each serve a role: during search, each new node is first quickly checked with point detection; when tracing back the entire path, line segment detection is used to verify connectivity.
3.4 Summary of This Chapter

This chapter constructs a three-dimensional simulation environment for a mountainous forest firefighting scenario. The terrain is generated by superimposing Gaussian functions to create eight hills, combined with Perlin noise for micro-relief. Threat sources include elliptical continuous fire fields, 85 tree obstacles (cylindrical trunk + spherical crown), and 100 shrub clumps (cylindrical). Comprehensive threat assessment uses the maximum value to avoid the illusion of threat cancellation due to summation. Collision detection is divided into point detection and line segment detection: the former for quick judgment during node expansion, the latter for safety verification during path backtracking. The environment model provides a test scenario for the improved A* algorithm in Chapter 4.
4. Improved A* Algorithm Design

When the traditional A* algorithm is directly applied to three-dimensional space, its performance is poor. Initially, attempts to search with a 6-neighborhood pattern yielded no improvement. Changing to a 26-neighborhood caused an explosion in the number of search nodes, all paths were composed of right-angle turns, and the algorithm was helpless in complex terrain. These various problems were difficulties encountered during experiments.

This chapter modifies and optimizes the A* algorithm, and the final version is called EA* (Enhanced A*) to distinguish it from other A* algorithms. Four modifications are interlinked. The 26-neighbor expansion handles search direction; 26 directions prevent the algorithm from being bound to coordinate axes. The adaptive step size based on grid lookup tables addresses the search granularity issue—taking large steps in open areas and small steps in dense forests. The dynamically weighted cost function balances efficiency and path quality. The path smoothing module converts polyline trajectories into smooth curves flyable by UAVs. These modifications are not arbitrary; without adaptive step size, the branching factor of 26-neighbor would cause search explosion; without dynamic weighting, large steps would overshoot in complex environments, missing optimal directions.
4.1 Algorithm Framework
4.1.1 Overall Design Idea and Flowchart of the Improved A* Algorithm
EA* inputs a three-dimensional grid map, start point Pstart, goal point Pgoal, and threat set T; the output is a smooth three-dimensional path P.

Before the search begins, the open list OPEN and closed list CLOSED are cleared. The start point is placed into OPEN with an initial cost f(Pstart) = h(Pstart). The main loop logic is not complicated; each time, the node n with the smallest f value is taken from OPEN. If n is sufficiently close to the goal (less than threshold εgoal), backtracking generates the initial path, and the process enters the post-processing phase.

If the goal is not yet reached, the algorithm takes a step from n in each of 26 directions. The step size is not fixed but determined by referencing the threat grid table. Each candidate neighbor must pass two checks: collision detection and boundary check. Passing nodes then have their comprehensive cost calculated. g(n) considers four items: flight distance, altitude change, threat exposure, and altitude preference. h(n) uses three-dimensional Euclidean distance, with weight wh dynamically adjusted as the search progresses.
After the search is complete, the path undergoes two post-processing steps: jump-point greedy simplification to remove redundant nodes, and B-spline curve generation to produce a smooth trajectory.

The reason for choosing a layered design here is not for the rigor of argument presentation. In reality, the paper comes from experimental exploration. Early mixed modules made debugging extremely difficult, so modules were split.
